perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees, during the period ending five years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3:
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder agrees, during the period ending ten years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3:
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-compete period to ten years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, depending on the specific facts and circumstances. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally disfavors non-compete agreements, and while there are some exceptions, a ten-year non-compete is likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly given that the contract is initially governed by California law while the Selling Shareholder is a resident of California. This directly contradicts the statute's prohibition on contracts restraining a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the non-compete period from five to ten years. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The increased duration of the non-compete period, especially in California where such agreements are generally disfavored, is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade, directly contradicting the statute's prohibition. The law snippet explicitly states that such restraints are void, and the modified text introduces a longer restraint.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that, at no time after the Effective Time for so long as Dollar Tree or its successors carry on the Business, shall Selling Shareholder or Selling Shareholder's Affiliates operate any business (a) under the names or names similar to "98 CENTS Clearance Center," "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers," "Everything 98 CENTS ... or less," or "Step Ahead
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that, at no time after the Effective Time shall Selling Shareholder or Selling Shareholder's Affiliates operate any business (a) under the names or names similar to "98 CENTS Clearance Center," "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers," "Everything 98 CENTS ... or less," or "Step Ahead
perturbation.explanation: Removing the qualifier 'for so long as Dollar Tree or its successors carry on the Business' creates an indefinite restriction on using certain names, potentially violating California's prohibition on unreasonable restraints of trade, even concerning trade names.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: By eliminating the condition that the restriction applies only as long as Dollar Tree operates the business, the modified text creates a perpetual ban on using those names, which is a much broader restriction and more likely to be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, conflicting with Section 16600.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the condition 'for so long as Dollar Tree or its successors carry on the Business,' which limits the duration of the restriction. By removing this, the restriction becomes indefinite. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The original contract limited the restraint to the period Dollar Tree or its successors operate the business. The modified contract creates a perpetual restraint, which is more likely to be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, thus contradicting Section 16600(a).

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that from July 22, 1998 until the end of the Restricted Period, Selling Shareholder has not and shall not, directly or indirectly, hire or attempt to hire any employee of Dollar Tree or SAI, or solicit, induce, or attempt to solicit or induce (other than through newspaper classified advertisements) any employee of Dollar Tree or SAI to leave his or her job for any reason whatsoever without the written consent of Dollar Tree. For purposes of this paragraph, an "employee" shall mean, as of any given date, anyone who has been an employee of Dollar Tree or SAI or any affiliate at any time during the four (4) month period prior to such date.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that from July 22, 1998 until the end of the Restricted Period, Selling Shareholder has not and shall not, directly or indirectly, hire or attempt to hire any employee of Dollar Tree or SAI, or solicit, induce, or attempt to solicit or induce any employee of Dollar Tree or SAI to leave his or her job for any reason whatsoever without the written consent of Dollar Tree. For purposes of this paragraph, an "employee" shall mean, as of any given date, anyone who has been an employee of Dollar Tree or SAI or any affiliate at any time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase '(other than through newspaper classified advertisements)' broadens the no-solicitation clause, potentially making it an unreasonable restraint on trade and thus violating California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the exception for newspaper advertisements broadens the restriction against soliciting employees, making it more likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law, which prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. This is because it restricts a wider range of activity related to potentially hiring former employees.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exception for newspaper classified advertisements, broadening the restriction against soliciting employees. This creates a contradiction as it potentially violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By removing the exception, the modified contract imposes a broader restraint on the Selling Shareholder's ability to engage in hiring, making it more likely to be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade. The original text permitted solicitation through newspaper ads, implicitly acknowledging a legitimate avenue for recruitment; removing this makes the restriction significantly more encompassing and thus more likely to be viewed as an unlawful restraint under California law.

perturbation.original_text: All property, materials, memoranda, notes, lists, records and other documents or papers (and all copies thereof), including such items stored in computer memories, on microfiche or by any other means, made or compiled by or on behalf of SAI or its affiliates relating to or useful in the operation of the Business, are and shall be SAI's property ("Property") and all such Property that is confidential or that SAI does not have copies of shall be delivered to SAI at or prior to the Effective Time; provided, however, Selling Shareholder shall be allowed to retain copies of such Property necessary for Selling Shareholder to prepare, compute or support Selling Shareholder's tax returns or tax liabilities.
perturbation.changed_text: All property, materials, memoranda, notes, lists, records and other documents or papers (and all copies thereof), including such items stored in computer memories, on microfiche or by any other means, made or compiled by or on behalf of SAI or its affiliates relating to or useful in the operation of the Business, are and shall be SAI's property ("Property") and all such Property that is confidential or that SAI does not have copies of shall be delivered to SAI at or prior to the Effective Time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the provision allowing the Selling Shareholder to retain copies of property necessary for tax purposes could violate California law regarding access to records needed for tax compliance. Under California law, individuals have a right to access documents necessary to fulfill their tax obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 19542
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the provision that allows the Selling Shareholder to keep copies of the property necessary for tax returns or tax liabilities, the agreement potentially prevents the Selling Shareholder from having access to documentation required for tax compliance under California law, which could run afoul of provisions ensuring taxpayers can access information related to their tax obligations as defined under CA Rev. & Tax Code § 19542.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, RTC 19542 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Revenue and Taxation Code - RTC DIVISION 2. OTHER TAXES [6001 - 61050] ( Heading of Division 2 amended by Stats. 1968, Ch. 279. ) PART 10.2. ADMINISTRATION OF FRANCHISE AND INCOME TAX LAWS [18401 - 19802] ( Part 10.2 added by Stats. 1993, Ch. 31, Sec. 26. ) CHAPTER 7. Administration of Tax [19501 - 19592] ( Chapter 7 added by Stats. 1993, Ch. 31, Sec. 26. ) ARTICLE 2. Disclosure of Information [19542 - 19572.5] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1993, Ch. 31, Sec. 26. ) 19542. Except as otherwise provided in this article and as required to administer Section 19005, it is a misdemeanor for the Franchise Tax Board or any member thereof, or any deputy, agent, clerk, or other officer or employee of the state, including its political subdivisions, or a jury commissioner, or any former officer or employee or other individual, who in the course of their employment or duty has or had access to returns, reports, or documents required to be filed under this part, to disclose or make known in any manner information as to the amount of income or any particulars, including the business affairs of a corporation, set forth or disclosed therein. (Amended by Stats. 2020, Ch. 230, Sec. 2. (SB 592) Effective January 1, 2021.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Selling Shareholder's ability to retain copies of property necessary for tax purposes, which directly contradicts the principle that taxpayers should have access to information related to their tax obligations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (RTC 19542) focuses on the confidentiality of tax information and prohibits its disclosure by state employees, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that removing the provision for retaining tax-related documents hinders the Selling Shareholder's ability to comply with tax laws, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially preventing access to necessary tax information. The original contract allowed access, and this has been revoked.
